name: The Train
image_prompt: Interior of a private salon car on the Trans-Siberian Railway at night, eastbound through winter taiga. Ornate wood-paneled walls in dark walnut with polished brass fittings and beaded trim. A long corridor of curtained windows, snow falling in horizontal white streaks past the glass, lit briefly by passing station lights. A Persian runner down the center aisle. Green-shaded brass banker's lamp on a writing desk. Warm amber wall sconces. Velvet-upholstered booth seating in burgundy. A silver samovar hissing on a side table. Wool coats and fur hats piled by the door. Empty, no people. Colors: dark walnut brown, polished brass gold, burgundy velvet, green shade, cold blue night outside. Belle-epoque Russian train atmosphere.
location: A private carriage on the Trans-Siberian Railway, eastbound from Moscow. The client chartered the last two cars: a salon car and a sleeper. The train left Yaroslavsky Station at 1 PM. The group will not arrive in Irkutsk for three days. The problem must be solved before they reach Lake Baikal.
time: 11 PM, night one. Moscow is six hours behind. The taiga is dark outside.
weather: Snow falling past the windows in horizontal streaks, lit briefly by station lights as the train passes through towns too small to stop for. Minus 22 outside. The carriage is warm. The glass fogs at the edges.

atmosphere.lighting: Warm amber sconces along the paneled walls of the salon car, brass fittings polished by a century of elbows. A green banker's lamp on the writing desk. The corridor between cars has a single bare bulb that swings with the train's rhythm, casting shadows that move like breathing.
atmosphere.sound: The deep metronomic clatter of wheels on track, metal on metal, steady enough to think by. The creak of wood paneling expanding and contracting. Wind against the windows when the train accelerates. A samovar hissing in the corner. Occasionally the horn, low and distant, for a crossing nobody sees.
atmosphere.crowd: A working group in a car that could hold more. The emptiness feels intentional. The provodnitsa brought tea at nine and will not return until six. The conductor checks tickets in the next car. Nobody else walks this corridor at night.
atmosphere.smell: Black tea, old wood polish, coal heat from the boiler, wool from heavy coats piled by the door, snow that melted off boots and seeped into the carpet, the faint diesel-and-iron smell that every Russian train carries like a signature

layout.salon_table: A long mahogany table bolted to the floor, leather chairs around it on brass rails that lock in place when the train rounds curves. The table surface vibrates with the track rhythm. A pen left unsupervised rolls east. Someone sits at the head facing forward. Someone else sits opposite, facing the direction the train came from. Everyone else fills in between. The arrangement is accidental but looks deliberate.
layout.writing_desk: A fold-down desk at the far end of the car, green lamp, inkwell that came with the car and has not been filled since the Soviet era. It was claimed immediately. A notebook lies open, pen moving in time with the train's sway, handwriting adapting to the curves.
layout.samovar_corner: A brass samovar built into a nook between the salon and the corridor, piped to the car's boiler. Glass cups in metal holders with sugar cubes in a chipped porcelain bowl. Someone stands here adding sugar one cube at a time, watching it dissolve, proving a point about marginal sweetness nobody asked about. Another refills a cup every twenty minutes like clockwork.
layout.observation_platform: An open-air vestibule at the rear of the last car, railed, exposed to the night. The cold is immediate and total. Someone stands here watching the tracks unwind behind the train, two steel lines narrowing to a point in the darkness. Another joins briefly, breath visible, studying the treeline. A third stands facing forward, leaning into the wind, because watching where you have been is the obvious choice and they refuse the obvious.
layout.corridor: A narrow wood-paneled passage connecting the salon to the sleeping compartments. Someone walks it back and forth, sixteen steps each way. The floor creaks on step nine. The count is being kept. Elsewhere, a notebook maps the corridor's connection to the rest of the train: nodes and edges, just like everything. Someone pauses by each window, looking out at the taiga passing in the dark, checking whether the map in their head matches the territory outside. It does not. It never does.

objects_of_note: The problem brief on the mahogany table, sliding imperceptibly east with the train's momentum. Someone put a tea glass on it as a weight. The condensation is soaking into the envelope.

A railway map of the Trans-Siberian route tacked above the writing desk. Moscow to Vladivostok, 9,288 kilometers. The stop at Irkutsk is circled in blue. A finger traced the route and said 'the system has exactly one path.' Another voice answered 'that is the constraint.'
The samovar, brass and dented, older than anyone in the car. A small plaque on its base reads a date in Cyrillic: 1952.
A gold bangle on the table, rolling gently with the train's rhythm, tracing small arcs on the mahogany. Someone watches it like a pendulum.

A window frosted at the edges, cleared in the center by someone's palm print. Through it, birch trees flash white in the train's light and disappear. They look like data points appearing and vanishing before you can record them.
mood: Linear and relentless. The train imposes a constraint no other room has: time is distance. Every minute of conversation burns a mile of track. The problem must be solved before Irkutsk or it does not get solved at all. The forward motion gives urgency to every framework. The clatter of the wheels is a clock that counts in kilometers instead of seconds. The taiga outside is endless and indifferent. The salon car is warm and finite. Minds in a box moving east at sixty miles per hour through the largest country on earth, carrying a problem that shrinks with every mile the train covers. The train does not stop for breakthroughs or dead ends. It only stops at Irkutsk.
